t Tuesday: Nickel prices shot up due to margin calls and forced buying by

      Nickel
   =20
 =20
The London Metal Exchange market for nickel futures broke last Tuesday: Nic=
kel prices shot up due to margin calls and forced buying by some big short =
traders, and in response the LME canceled $3.9 billion of trades, rolled ba=
ck the price to pretend the Tuesday morning session had never happened, and=
 shut down trading for a week. (We talked about this  here and  here.)This =
morning the LME reopened for nickel trading, but quite gingerly. LME announ=
ced that it would only allow the price to move up or down by 5% from its pr=
evious close. That close was itself=C2=A0sort of artificial (since it ignor=
ed all the canceled trades from Tuesday).This was arguably a strange thing =
to do. The reason that LME suspended trading and canceled trades is that=C2=
=A0it thought (and many traders thought) that the market price of nickel fu=
tures did not reflect the =E2=80=9Creal=E2=80=9D value of nickel, because o=
f, essentially, glitches in the financing of some big nickel traders. The p=
rices =E2=80=9Cwere becoming disconnected from, I believe, physical reality=
,=E2=80=9D  said the head of the LME.Arguably the point of taking=C2=A0a wh=
ole week=C2=A0off trading was to let investors think about things, line up =
the appropriate financing, and come back ready to move nickel to the right =
price. There is no reason to think that last Monday=E2=80=99s closing price=
 of $48,078 per ton was the =E2=80=9Cright=E2=80=9D=C2=A0price; that price =
already reflected some dislocation from short squeezes, and was up 66% from=
 the previous week=E2=80=99s close. (It also did not reflect real trades th=
at happened on Tuesday morning and then got canceled.) The LME might have s=
aid =E2=80=9Cright, we are going to give everyone a week to get their finan=
cing in order so that=C2=A0no one is forced to buy or sell due to margin ca=
lls, and then we=E2=80=99re going to let the market open and move back to t=
he real price, no matter what that is, but probably a lot lower than $48,00=
0.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0And in fact the nickel news over the last week pretty much=
 has been about how the trader with the biggest short position =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0Chinese nickel tycoon Xiang Guangda=E2=80=99s company, Tsingshan Holding=
 Group Co. =E2=80=94=C2=A0has been lining up financing to avoid getting for=
ced out of its short. Which was not that hard to do, because Tsingshan=C2=
=A0produces=C2=A0tons of nickel, so as the price of nickel goes up (and it =
loses money on its short) the value of its assets goes up. So banks should =
be happy to lend Tsingshan lots of money to, essentially, hedge its nickel =
production, and in fact the banks did agree to do that.=C2=A0More generally=
, the point of shutting down an exchange when prices move too=C2=A0far is t=
hat you think that those prices probably do not reflect fundamental supply =
and demand. At any given moment on any given exchange, there are only some =
traders trading; they have risk limits and financing constraints, and if th=
e price gets out of whack they might just turn off their computers and go h=
ome. And then whoever=E2=80=99s left can move the price in weird ways. But =
when the exchange shuts down, it (1) pauses trading, so there are no more w=
eird price moves, and (2) sends a strong signal=C2=A0to the market =E2=80=
=9CHEY THINGS ARE MESSED UP, COME GET SOME FREE MONEY.=E2=80=9D Anyone who =
wasn=E2=80=99t=C2=A0hanging around at the time of the shutdown =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0banks, hedge funds, nickel producers, industrial users, anyone with an i=
nterest in nickel prices =E2=80=94 now has an opportunity to show up. So wh=
en the market=C2=A0reopens, after a long enough time, everyone who thought =
=E2=80=9Chey that price is way too high (or low)=E2=80=9D can show up and s=
ell (or buy)=C2=A0and the price will get back to normal.It is not just Tsin=
gshan, by the way. Here is  a Bloomberg News story about how =E2=80=9CTrafi=
gura Group, one of the world=E2=80=99s top oil and metals traders, has been=
 holding talks with private equity groups to secure additional financing as=
 soaring prices trigger giant margin calls across the commodities industry.=
=E2=80=9D I don=E2=80=99t think that the right reading of that story is =E2=
=80=9CTrafigura, a huge commodity trader, has lost a bunch of money on comm=
odity volatility and needs more money.=E2=80=9D That=E2=80=99s possible, bu=
t in general big traders do=C2=A0well in periods of volatility. Instead the=
 story is more likely to be something like =E2=80=9CTrafigura is making ton=
s of money in this volatile commodity environment, but it needs lots of fin=
ancing to keep doing that.=E2=80=9D (=E2=80=9CNew sources of funding could =
give Trafigura firepower to take advantage of market opportunities even at =
high price levels,=E2=80=9D says Bloomberg News.) When prices are dislocate=
d, there is a lot of money to be made, but you need a lot of financing to m=
ake it.[1]Given that dynamic it might have made sense for the LME to just r=
eopen without any price limits: =E2=80=9CStuff got real weird last week, so=
 we=E2=80=99re going to forget about it and start over from the beginning; =
whatever you want to pay for nickel now, after a week of careful reflection=
,=C2=A0is going to be the price.=E2=80=9D With enough notice that might get=
 you a good price, probably better than 5% up or down from last Monday=E2=
=80=99s somewhat dislocated price.But, whatever, LME came back with a 5% pr=
ice band, and then promptly (1) did some trades outside of the price band a=
nd (2) canceled them retroactively, oops oops oops oops oops:As detailed in=
 Notice 22/064, the LME reopened the Nickel market at 0800 (with the pre-op=
en starting at 0730) on 16 March 2022. As the market opened, the uncrossing=
 algorithm discovered an opening price of US$45,590 (which was the lower da=
ily price limit, i.e. 5% below the prices published in Notice 22/067) for 3=
-month Nickel. Unfortunately due to a systems error, LMEselect then allowed=
 a small number of trades to be executed below this lower daily price limit=
.The LME immediately decided to suspend Nickel trading on LMEselect while t=
he system error is investigated.All Nickel trades executed on LMEselect at =
the lower daily price limit will remain. The small number of Nickel trades =
executed on LMEselect below the lower daily price limit will be cancelled.O=
h, nickel. Anyway it=E2=80=99s  fixed now and the market reopened. The LME =
will not, however, generate an =E2=80=9COfficial Price=E2=80=9D for nickel =
for some time:A key element of the day=E2=80=99s activity is discovery of t=
he Official Prices, which are used by the industrial metals community aroun=
d the world. ...Having previously held discussions with the physical indust=
ry, the LME had announced (in Notice 22/064 in particular paragraph 25) tha=
t it would not publish Official Prices if the market had reached its daily =
price limit during the period within which the relevant price is being dete=
rmined on a given Business Day. This is because physical users preferred no=
t to have Official Prices which they had been unable to trade (which would =
be the case if the price were at the daily price limit).The daily price lim=
its have been constructed in order to help the Nickel price to find its nat=
ural level over the coming days in an orderly fashion. The LME will commenc=
e publishing Nickel Official Prices as soon as these are discovered without=
 a daily price limit being engaged during the relevant Ring trading session=
.Basically the LME is set up to produce the wrong price for nickel for the =
next few days, in order to slowly ease into the right price. Seems like an =
odd choice.

      Russia default
   =20
 =20
Today Russia is supposed to pay international=C2=A0bondholders $117 million=
 of interest=C2=A0on its dollar-denominated bonds. Russia=E2=80=99s central=
 bank and ministry of finance are=C2=A0subject to U.S. and international sa=
nctions, meaning among other things that their=C2=A0 dollar accounts are fr=
ozen. So it seems like Russia can tell its U.S. banks to send dollars to it=
s bondholders, but the dollars are frozen and might not go to bondholders:R=
ussia=E2=80=99s finance minister Anton Siluanov said on Wednesday that the =
payment instruction had been sent to the US bank that usually handles such =
transactions but that there was a risk the cash would not get through, acco=
rding to state newswire Ria Novosti. Earlier this week Siluanov said that p=
ayment will be made in roubles if the transfer is unsuccessful, adding that=
 western sanctions freezing some of the Russian central bank=E2=80=99s asse=
ts were an attempt to force the country into an =E2=80=9Cartificial default=
=E2=80=9D on its $38.5bn of foreign currency bonds.If Russia can=E2=80=99t =
pay in dollars and has to pay in rubles, does that count as a default? Yes,=
 is the traditional answer; that is what  Fitch Ratings has said, and presu=
mably what other ratings agencies will say, and also effectively what credi=
t default swap contracts say.[2] I am not sure how much this matters, insof=
ar as, if Russia defaults, there is  not that much its bondholders can do a=
bout it. The traditional remedies include (1) suing (which will get you a j=
udgment that you can=E2=80=99t enforce), (2) going to Moscow to negotiate s=
ome sort of restructuring (which you can=E2=80=99t do, due to the sanctions=
) and (3) shouting =E2=80=9Cif you default you will be locked out of intern=
ational capital markets!=E2=80=9D (which has already happened).=C2=A0Also t=
o the extent Russia pays its foreign bondholders in rubles =E2=80=A6=C2=A0c=
an they =E2=80=A6=C2=A0get the rubles? In addition to the dollar bonds with=
 payments due today, Russia has ruble-denominated bonds held by foreign inv=
estors that had interest payments due two weeks ago. Russia =E2=80=9Cpaid=
=E2=80=9D those coupons, in some abstract sense, but the payments  did not =
get to investors:Fitch=E2=80=99s latest release Tuesday also noted Russia=
=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9Cfailure to credit non-resident investors=E2=80=9D with =
the coupons for Russian local-currency government bonds, known as OFZs, whi=
ch were due on March 2.=E2=80=9CWe understand that Russia=E2=80=99s Ministr=
y of Finance made these coupon payments on the 2024 OFZs to the National Se=
ttlement Depository, but they were not paid on to foreign investors because=
 of Central Bank of Russia restrictions,=E2=80=9D according to Fitch. =E2=
=80=9CThis will constitute a default if not cured within 30 days of the pay=
ments falling due.=E2=80=9DThis seems to be mostly due to a Russian =E2=80=
=9Ccentral bank ban on sending foreign currency abroad,=E2=80=9D though I s=
uppose if you=E2=80=99re a U.S. investor or intermediary you might worry a =
little bit about=C2=A0U.S.=C2=A0sanctions restricting your ability to take =
or pass along=C2=A0ruble payments from Russia. But broadly speaking the poi=
nt seems to be:=09The U.S. might not allow Russia to pay its bonds in dolla=
rs, and=09Russia might not=C2=A0allow its bondholders to get paid in rubles=
.Seems like a default! In some sense the default is  imminent and historic:=
The country would be in default if it hasn=E2=80=99t, within 30 days of a M=
arch 2 payment due date, =E2=80=9Ccured=E2=80=9D its obligations with regar=
d to ruble-denominated bond coupons that were due earlier this month, Fitch=
 said in a document Tuesday on how to understand potential sovereign defaul=
t events.=C2=A0That sets April 1 as a potential key date for the country, w=
hich hasn=E2=80=99t defaulted on local currency bonds since 1998 and last f=
ailed to meet foreign-debt obligations back in 1918, following the Bolshevi=
k revolution.In another sense the default has already happened: Once sancti=
ons made it impossible for many international investors to trade with Russi=
a, normal concepts like =E2=80=9Ca default will drive down the price of bon=
ds=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Ca default will cut Russia off from international fi=
nancing=E2=80=9D=C2=A0happened immediately, so the eventual=C2=A0actual=C2=
=A0default has limited economic impact. And the usual=C2=A0resolution=C2=A0=
of a default =E2=80=94=C2=A0the defaulting country restructures its bonds, =
or disavows them, and enough time passes that it eventually can come back t=
o the market =E2=80=94=C2=A0seems impossible until=C2=A0Russia's invasion o=
f=C2=A0Ukraine is resolved first. In a sense today (or April 1) is an impor=
tant day, but in another sense Russia defaulted on its bonds the minute it =
invaded Ukraine and will be in default until the invasion ends.Elsewhere Ru=
ssia has a lot of gold, but  how useful is that?Bank of Russia expanded its=
 gold reserves almost sixfold since the mid-2000s, creating the world=E2=80=
=99s fifth-biggest stockpile that=E2=80=99s valued at about $140 billion. I=
t=E2=80=99s the type of asset it could sell to shore up the ruble, which ha=
s plunged as global economies isolate Russia following its invasion of Ukra=
ine.Doing so will be difficult. Sanctions forbid U.S., U.K. and European Un=
ion institutions from doing business with Russia=E2=80=99s central bank. Tr=
aders and banks are wary of buying the country=E2=80=99s bullion indirectly=
 or using other currencies out of fear of reputational damage or falling fo=
ul of penalties. And senators in Washington want secondary sanctions on any=
one buying or selling Russian gold.=E2=80=9CThis is why they bought their g=
old, it was for a situation just like this,=E2=80=9D said Fergal O=E2=80=99=
Connor, a lecturer at Cork University Business School. =E2=80=9CBut if no o=
ne will trade it with you, it doesn=E2=80=99t matter.=E2=80=9DThey could pa=
y the coupons in gold? =E2=80=9CSend us a mailing address and we=E2=80=99ll=
 ship you the gold=E2=80=9D? I don=E2=80=99t know, probably not a great ide=
a.

   =20
      ESG
   =20
 =20
If you are an investor interested in environmental, social and governance i=
ssues, you have had to make some choices about Russia in recent weeks. Mayb=
e not that many choices. Various Russian entities were subject to sanctions=
, many=C2=A0Russian securities were suspended in Europe and the U.S. and re=
moved from indexes, the whole Moscow=C2=A0Exchange has been  shut down for =
weeks; it=E2=80=99s possible that for many of your Russian positions you ha=
d no choice at all beyond =E2=80=9Cwe have to sell this but also we can=E2=
=80=99t sell it.=E2=80=9DStill, in theory, as an ESG investor, you probably=
 want to have a view on investing in (non-sanctioned, tradable) Russian ass=
ets. =E2=80=9CInvesting in Russia supports a war of aggression against Ukra=
ine so we won=E2=80=99t do it=E2=80=9D seems like the leading ESG view righ=
t now, though I suppose others are also possible.But how far do you take th=
at view? If Russian companies are off-limits for ESG investors, what about =
companies that invest in Russia? Do business in Russia? Buy Russian oil? If=
 you are an ESG investor that spends time (1) evaluating Western companies =
and investing only in the ones that pollute the least, have the best social=
 policies, etc. and (2) pressuring the companies in which you do invest to =
improve their social and environmental policies =E2=80=94=C2=A0have you add=
ed Russia to the list of criteria? Do you now refuse to invest in companies=
 that do business in Russia? Do you measure each company=E2=80=99s Russia e=
xposure, rank companies within each industry, and refuse to invest in the c=
ompanies in the top quartile of Russia exposure within their sector? When y=
our stewardship team meets with companies=E2=80=99 managers, do they now sa=
y =E2=80=9Calso stop doing business with Russia=E2=80=9D?You could go furth=
er. Are you developing policies on whatever the Russia equivalent of =E2=80=
=9C greenwashing=E2=80=9D is, where when companies say =E2=80=9Cwe have div=
ested from Russia=E2=80=9D you can say =E2=80=9Cah no you=E2=80=99ve made s=
ome big headline moves but you=E2=80=99re actually still doing a lot in Rus=
sia, we are not giving you ESG credit for this=E2=80=9D? Are you developing=
 a sense of, as it were, Scope 1, 2 and 3 Russia exposure, where you evalua=
te companies not only on how much business they do in Russia but also how m=
uch business their suppliers and customers do in Russia?=C2=A0The point is =
that there is a huge=C2=A0mechanism=C2=A0of ESG investing, all sorts of met=
hodologies and standard-setters and consultants and approaches that ESG inv=
estors and regulators and third parties have built up, over the years, to a=
ddress =E2=80=94=C2=A0mainly carbon emissions, really, but also I suppose o=
ther big social factors. And now Russia has become, almost overnight, a hug=
e ESG issue. Can you fit it into the existing mechanisms? Can you build new=
 mechanisms on the fly? Will everyone =E2=80=94=C2=A0or every ESG investor =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0agree on what the mechanisms are?Anyway  here=E2=80=99s the =
Church of England:TotalEnergies SE has been warned by two U.K. shareholders=
 that they would divest their stakes if the French company fails to exit it=
s businesses in Russia.=C2=A0The Church of England Pensions Board and the C=
hurch Commissioners for England are turning up the heat on the energy major=
=E2=80=99s Chief Executive Officer Patrick Pouyanne, a sign of the pressure=
 companies are facing to isolate Russian President Vladimir Putin=E2=80=99s=
 regime. TotalEnergies has said it will stop buying Russian crude and inves=
ting in new projects in the country, but it continues to hold stakes in Nov=
atek PJSC and other projects. =E2=80=A6Russia=E2=80=99s invasion of Ukraine=
 shows =E2=80=9Cit is clear that TotalEnergies=E2=80=99 position is incompa=
tible with maintaining a social license to operate,=E2=80=9D the heads of r=
esponsible investment at the two U.K. funds wrote in a joint letter to Pouy=
anne dated Tuesday. =E2=80=9CWe would underline the seriousness of our requ=
est and that we will need to consider our position as shareholders in Total=
Energies if further steps are not forthcoming.=E2=80=9D

      AMC Gold
   =20
 =20
Yesterday AMC Entertainment Holdings Inc., the operator of movie theaters a=
nd a meme stock, announced that it was buying 22% of a small gold miner cal=
led Hycroft Mining Holding Corp. Weird stuff!  I suggested that the way to =
understand the deal might be that AMC is in the business of raising money a=
s a meme stock and, now, helping other companies raise money as meme stocks=
:AMC=E2=80=99s core competency ...=C2=A0is using the meme-stock mechanism t=
o raise money, and now it is in the business of selling that expertise to o=
ther companies. AMC is=C2=A0an investment bank, or really a merchant bank t=
hat helps small companies=C2=A0do meme-driven at-the-market offerings and t=
akes equity for its fee.=C2=A0Here are  Bloomberg News and Wall Street Jour=
nal stories=C2=A0about how the deal came together that sort of confirm that=
 model. Jason Mudrick, the hedge fund manager  who =E2=80=9Cwas an architec=
t of AMC=E2=80=99s narrow escape from bankruptcy last year=E2=80=9D and who=
 bought a bunch of AMC shares last year to sell into retail enthusiasm, is =
also a big holder of Hycroft stock (and took Hycroft public through his spe=
cial purpose acquisition company). So:Jason Mudrick, a hedge-fund manager a=
nd one of Hycroft=E2=80=99s top investors, saw an opportunity for the compa=
ny to raise much-needed funding, people familiar with the matter said. It w=
as down to just $8 million in cash. =E2=80=A6Mr. Mudrick called [AMC Chief =
Executive Officer Adam]=C2=A0Aron last week and asked if he could offer Hyc=
roft CEO Diane Garrett advice on how to tap equity funding through the type=
 of at-the-market share offering that AMC had successfully used, people fam=
iliar with the matter said. =E2=80=A6After Mr. Mudrick asked for advice on =
a Hycroft stock offering, Mr. Aron became intrigued and said that AMC was i=
nterested in investing in Hycroft itself, people familiar with the matter s=
aid. Mr. Aron had never heard of Hycroft before Mr. Mudrick called him.I su=
ppose if you were starting a chain of movie theaters you might call Adam Ar=
on for advice, but he might not give it to you for competitive reasons, and=
 anyway it=E2=80=99s not like he=E2=80=99s had a perfect record of=C2=A0run=
ning movie theaters profitably in recent years. But if you were launching a=
n at-the-market offering into a rising market for your stock driven by reta=
il enthusiasm, you would=C2=A0absolutely=C2=A0call Adam Aron for advice; he=
 has been=C2=A0remarkably=C2=A0successful at that. It makes sense that he w=
ould want to share his wisdom, but also get a cut of the upside.

      Blockchain blockchain blockchain
   =20
 =20
Just kidding! For a while, like five years ago, you could not go five minut=
es in financial services without hearing the word =E2=80=9Cblockchain.=E2=
=80=9D Every financial product would one day trade on =E2=80=9Cthe blockcha=
in,=E2=80=9D which would revolutionize settlement and lead to huge efficien=
cy gains. Everything would live on the blockchain and be instantly legible =
to everyone at all times; every trade would settle instantly; vast realms o=
f back-office processes would disappear and be replaced by a single distrib=
uted ledger.The product that would most especially live on the blockchain=
=C2=A0was leveraged loans. Leveraged loans are strange in that (1) they are=
 a large, important,=C2=A0frequently traded asset class, (2) they are kind =
of like high-yield bonds, which trade and settle in a pretty normal way, bu=
t (3) for various historical and legal reasons they do=C2=A0not=C2=A0settle=
 like bonds; instead they can take two weeks to settle and sometimes you ha=
ve to use a fax machine. Putting stock trading on the blockchain might add =
some efficiency, but putting leveraged loans on the blockchain would add a=
=C2=A0ton=C2=A0of efficiency. Also millions of individuals trade stocks, wh=
ile a relatively small pool of banks and investors trade leveraged loans, s=
o it is just a bit easier to get everyone to agree to migrate them to the b=
lockchain. Leveraged loans were the lowest-hanging blockchain fruit, and pe=
ople talked constantly about moving them to the blockchain.Now it is 2022 a=
nd here is Bloomberg=E2=80=99s Paula Seligson with =E2=80=9C Old-School Lev=
eraged Loan Market Steps Closer to Ditching Faxes=E2=80=9D:The $1.38 trilli=
on U.S. leveraged loan market is nearing an innovation that could finally s=
hift back-office operations to a centralized system and away from investors=
 having to manually track their positions -- a process that can still inclu=
de the occasional fax.Bank of America Corp., Citigroup Inc. and JPMorgan Ch=
ase & Co. on Wednesday unveiled the name of the new platform that will let =
lenders access data across their portfolios in one place and said that Cred=
it Suisse Group AG, one of the top arrangers for leveraged loans, has joine=
d the portal.Called Versana, the platform will formally launch around the m=
iddle of this year with term loans, and then soon add revolving credit faci=
lities and other services, said the company=E2=80=99s Chief Executive Offic=
er Cynthia Sachs. =E2=80=A6Every loan has one bank that serves as the admin=
istrative agent and provides back-office record keeping. They notify lender=
s on a range of changes that can occur, such as an interest payment, a comp=
any paying down debt early and amendments to the legal documentation.But th=
ose notifications are not standardized, and investors often have to track a=
 hodgepodge of messages from a number of different banks. If there is a dis=
crepancy, an investor has to call up, email or instant message the bank to =
sort out why, for example, the cash flows in their portfolio don=E2=80=99t =
match their expectations.=C2=A0No mention of blockchains! A centralized sys=
tem! To be fair this is for administrative notices, not settlement, but you=
 get the idea. =E2=80=9CWe should have a nicer and more logical way to trac=
k leveraged loans by computer; these faxes are dumb,=E2=80=9D people have t=
hought for a long time. In 2017 they expressed that sentiment by saying =E2=
=80=9Ceverything will move to the blockchain.=E2=80=9D Now they just build =
a computer system.

<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Nickel</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The London Metal Exchange market for n=
ickel futures broke last Tuesday: Nickel prices shot up due to margin calls=
 and forced buying by some big short traders, and in response the LME cance=
led $3.9 billion of trades, rolled back the price to pretend the Tuesday mo=
rning session had never happened, and shut down trading for a week. (We tal=
ked about this <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/270=
48155.256604/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMj=
ItMDMtMDgvbmlja2VsLWlzLWNhbmNlbGVkP2NtcGlkPUJCRDAzMTYyMl9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0b=
V9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIyMDMxNiZ1dG1f=
Y2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB09a41c98" itemprop=3D"S=
toryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000=
; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom=
-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> here=
</a> and <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27048155.=
256604/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMt=
MTQvbmlja2VsLWJsb3d1cC1tYWRlLWEtbG90LW9mLXRyb3VibGU_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDMxNjIyX01=
PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm=
09MjIwMzE2JnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB1be8e04=
a" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;"> here</a>.)</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This morning the L=
ME reopened for nickel trading, but quite gingerly. LME <a href=3D"https://=
link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27048155.256604/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubG1lL=
mNvbS9hcGkvc2l0ZWNvcmUvTWVtYmVyTm90aWNlc1NlYXJjaEFwaS9Eb3dubG9hZD9pZD1hYzU2=
NGE2Yi1iNzBmLTRhZTktOWYwZC1kOWU1ZGVjZGVhNmU/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBdc0552=
44" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;">announced</a> that it would only allow the price to move up or=
 down by 5% from its previous close. That close was itself=C2=A0sort of art=
ificial (since it ignored all the canceled trades from Tuesday).</p><p styl=
e=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This was arguably a strange thing to do. The reason t=
hat LME suspended trading and canceled trades is that=C2=A0it thought (and =
many traders thought) that the market price of nickel futures did not refle=
ct the =E2=80=9Creal=E2=80=9D value of nickel, because of, essentially, gli=
tches in the financing of some big nickel traders. The prices =E2=80=9Cwere=
 becoming disconnected from, I believe, physical reality,=E2=80=9D <a href=
=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27048155.256604/aHR0cHM6L=
y93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMTQvaW5zaWRlLW5pY2tl=
bC1zLXNob3J0LXNxdWVlemUtaG93LXByaWNlLXN1cmdlcy1oYWx0ZWQtbG1lLXRyYWRpbmc_Y21=
waWQ9QkJEMDMxNjIyX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3=
NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjIwMzE2JnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b=
8524a319a23cB8385baae" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" targe=
t=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bor=
der-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: s=
olid; background-color: #ccc;"> said the head of the LME</a>.</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Arguably the point of taking=C2=A0<em>a whole week=C2=
=A0</em>off trading was to let investors think about things, line up the ap=
propriate financing, and come back ready to move nickel to the right price.=
 There is no reason to think that last Monday=E2=80=99s closing price of $4=
8,078 per ton was the =E2=80=9Cright=E2=80=9D=C2=A0price; that price alread=
y reflected some dislocation from short squeezes, and was up 66% from the p=
revious week=E2=80=99s close. (It also did not reflect real trades that hap=
pened on Tuesday morning and then got canceled.) The LME might have said =
=E2=80=9Cright, we are going to give everyone a week to get their financing=
 in order so that=C2=A0no one is forced to buy or sell due to margin calls,=
 and then we=E2=80=99re going to let the market open and move back to the r=
eal price, no matter what that is, but probably a lot lower than $48,000.=
=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And in fact the nickel news=
 over the last week pretty much has been about how the trader with the bigg=
est short position =E2=80=94=C2=A0Chinese nickel tycoon Xiang Guangda=E2=80=
=99s company, Tsingshan Holding Group Co. =E2=80=94=C2=A0has been lining up=
 financing to avoid getting forced out of its short. Which was not that har=
d to do, because Tsingshan=C2=A0<em>produces=C2=A0</em>tons of nickel, so a=
s the price of nickel goes up (and it loses money on its short) the value o=
f its assets goes up. So banks should be happy to lend Tsingshan lots of mo=
ney to, essentially, hedge its nickel production, and in fact the banks <a =
href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27048155.256604/aHR0c=
HM6Ly93d3cuc3BnbG9iYWwuY29tL2NvbW1vZGl0eS1pbnNpZ2h0cy9lbi9tYXJrZXQtaW5zaWdo=
dHMvbGF0ZXN0LW5ld3MvbWV0YWxzLzAzMTUyMi1jaGluYXMtdHNpbmdzaGFuLWVudGVycy1kZWF=
sLXdpdGgtYmFua2Vycy10by1yZXNvbHZlLW5pY2tlbC10cmFkZS1wb3NpdGlvbg/607f07ceef4=
b8524a319a23cB58572930" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" targ=
et=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bo=
rder-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: =
solid; background-color: #ccc;">did agree to do that</a>.=C2=A0</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">More generally, the point of shutting down an exchange=
 when prices move too=C2=A0far is that you think that those prices probably=
 do not reflect fundamental supply and demand. At any given moment on any g=
iven exchange, there are only some traders trading; they have risk limits a=
nd financing constraints, and if the price gets out of whack they might jus=
t turn off their computers and go home. And then whoever=E2=80=99s left can=
 move the price in weird ways. But when the exchange shuts down, it (1) pau=
ses trading, so there are no more weird price moves, and (2) sends a strong=
 signal=C2=A0to the market =E2=80=9CHEY THINGS ARE MESSED UP, COME GET SOME=
 FREE MONEY.=E2=80=9D Anyone who <em>wasn=E2=80=99t=C2=A0</em>hanging aroun=
d at the time of the shutdown =E2=80=94=C2=A0banks, hedge funds, nickel pro=
ducers, industrial users, anyone with an interest in nickel prices =E2=80=
=94 now has an opportunity to show up. So when the market=C2=A0<em>reopens<=
/em>, after a long enough time, everyone who thought =E2=80=9Chey that pric=
e is way too high (or low)=E2=80=9D can show up and sell (or buy)=C2=A0and =
the price will get back to normal.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It is no=
t just Tsingshan, by the way. Here is <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomber=
gbusiness.com/click/27048155.256604/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXd=
zL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMTUvdHJhZmlndXJhLXNlZWtzLWZ1bmRpbmctZnJvbS1wcml2YX=
RlLWVxdWl0eS1hcy1jb21tb2RpdGllcy1zb2FyP2NtcGlkPUJCRDAzMTYyMl9NT05FWVNUVUZGJ=
nV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIyMDMxNiZ1=
dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB5fad6cd8" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
 a Bloomberg News story</a> about how =E2=80=9CTrafigura Group, one of the =
world=E2=80=99s top oil and metals traders, has been holding talks with pri=
vate equity groups to secure additional financing as soaring prices trigger=
 giant margin calls across the commodities industry.=E2=80=9D I don=E2=80=
=99t think that the right reading of that story is =E2=80=9CTrafigura, a hu=
ge commodity trader, has lost a bunch of money on commodity volatility and =
needs more money.=E2=80=9D That=E2=80=99s possible, but in general big trad=
ers do=C2=A0<em>well </em>in periods of volatility. Instead the story is mo=
re likely to be something like =E2=80=9CTrafigura is making tons of money i=
n this volatile commodity environment, but it needs lots of financing to ke=
ep doing that.=E2=80=9D (=E2=80=9CNew sources of funding could give Trafigu=
ra firepower to take advantage of market opportunities even at high price l=
evels,=E2=80=9D says Bloomberg News.) When prices are dislocated, there is =
a lot of money to be made, but you need a lot of financing to make it.[1]</=
p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Given that dynamic it might have made sense =
for the LME to just reopen without any price limits: =E2=80=9CStuff got rea=
l weird last week, so we=E2=80=99re going to forget about it and start over=
 from the beginning; whatever you want to pay for nickel now, after a week =
of careful reflection,=C2=A0is going to be the price.=E2=80=9D With enough =
notice that might get you a good price, probably better than 5% up or down =
from last Monday=E2=80=99s somewhat dislocated price.</p><p style=3D"margin=
: 16px 0;">But, whatever, LME came back with a 5% price band, and then prom=
ptly (1) did some trades outside of the price band and (2) canceled them re=
troactively, oops oops oops <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.=
com/click/27048155.256604/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cubG1lLmNvbS9hcGkvc2l0ZWNvcmUvTWVtY=
mVyTm90aWNlc1NlYXJjaEFwaS9Eb3dubG9hZD9pZD04YjNiMDQwNi00YWYzLTQ0NTgtYTE1Mi00=
MzdhZjEyNmM1YWY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cC1136e96b" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" i=
temscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-deco=
ration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #00=
0000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">oops oops</a>:</=
p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">As detailed in Notice 22/064, th=
e LME reopened the Nickel market at 0800 (with the pre-open starting at 073=
0) on 16 March 2022. As the market opened, the uncrossing algorithm discove=
red an opening price of US$45,590 (which was the lower daily price limit, i=
.e. 5% below the prices published in Notice 22/067) for 3-month Nickel. Unf=
ortunately due to a systems error, LMEselect then allowed a small number of=
 trades to be executed below this lower daily price limit.</p><p style=3D"m=
argin: 16px 0;">The LME immediately decided to suspend Nickel trading on LM=
Eselect while the system error is investigated.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px=
 0;">All Nickel trades executed on LMEselect at the lower daily price limit=
 will remain. The small number of Nickel trades executed on LMEselect below=
 the lower daily price limit will be cancelled.</p></blockquote><p style=3D=
"margin: 16px 0;">Oh, nickel. Anyway it=E2=80=99s <a href=3D"https://link.m=
ail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27048155.256604/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJ=
nLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMTYvbG1lLXNheXMtZWxlY3Ryb25pYy10cmFkaW=
5nLWluLW5pY2tlbC10by1yZW9wZW4tYWZ0ZXItZ2xpdGNoP2NtcGlkPUJCRDAzMTYyMl9NT05FW=
VNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIy=
MDMxNiZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBe928204f" i=
temprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"co=
lor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; b=
order-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: =
#ccc;"> fixed now</a> and the market reopened. The LME will not, however, <=
a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27048155.256604/aHR=
0cHM6Ly93d3cubG1lLmNvbS9hcGkvc2l0ZWNvcmUvTWVtYmVyTm90aWNlc1NlYXJjaEFwaS9Eb3=
dubG9hZD9pZD0yMzNhMDdhOC0yOWYxLTRiMDQtOTFkZS05NjA5MjFkYzIwOGI/607f07ceef4b8=
524a319a23cD2c395f61" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=
=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;">generate an =E2=80=9COfficial Price=E2=80=9D<=
/a> for nickel for some time:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A=
 key element of the day=E2=80=99s activity is discovery of the Official Pri=
ces, which are used by the industrial metals community around the world. ..=
.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Having previously held discussions with t=
he physical industry, the LME had announced (in Notice 22/064 in particular=
 paragraph 25) that it would not publish Official Prices if the market had =
reached its daily price limit during the period within which the relevant p=
rice is being determined on a given Business Day. This is because physical =
users preferred not to have Official Prices which they had been unable to t=
rade (which would be the case if the price were at the daily price limit).<=
/p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The daily price limits have been constructe=
d in order to help the Nickel price to find its natural level over the comi=
ng days in an orderly fashion. The LME will commence publishing Nickel Offi=
cial Prices as soon as these are discovered without a daily price limit bei=
ng engaged during the relevant Ring trading session.</p></blockquote><p sty=
le=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Basically the LME is set up to produce the wrong pri=
ce for nickel for the next few days, in order to slowly ease into the right=
 price. Seems like an odd choice.</p><table class=3D"liveintent-sponsored-c=
ontent" width=3D"auto" cellpadding=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0">

5px;">Russia default</h2>
</td>

</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Today Russia is supposed to pay intern=
ational=C2=A0bondholders $117 million of interest=C2=A0on its dollar-denomi=
nated bonds. Russia=E2=80=99s central bank and ministry of finance are=C2=
=A0subject to <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/2704=
8155.256604/aHR0cHM6Ly9ob21lLnRyZWFzdXJ5Lmdvdi9uZXdzL3ByZXNzLXJlbGVhc2VzL2p=

=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">U.S.</a> and internation=
al sanctions, meaning among other things that their=C2=A0<a href=3D"https:/=
/link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27048155.256604/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxv=
b21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDItMjgvcnVzc2lhLXMtbW9uZXktaXM=
tZ29uZT9jbXBpZD1CQkQwMzE2MjJfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3=
VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMjAzMTYmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/6=
07f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB7d1b81ab" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"items=
cope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !imp=
ortant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bott=
om-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> dollar accounts are frozen</a>. =
So it seems like Russia can tell its U.S. banks to send dollars to its bond=
holders, but <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27048=
155.256604/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZnQuY29tL2NvbnRlbnQvMmMwZDdhOGItYTQ4Yi00Mjg3LWFj=
M2EtMzc2NjAzMzQ3YmEz/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBd9eef396" itemprop=3D"StoryLi=
nk" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text=
-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color=
: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">the dollars=
 are frozen and might not go to bondholders</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D=
"margin: 16px 0;">Russia=E2=80=99s finance minister Anton Siluanov said on =
Wednesday that the payment instruction had been sent to the US bank that us=
ually handles such transactions but that there was a risk the cash would no=
t get through, according to state newswire Ria Novosti. Earlier this week S=
iluanov said that payment will be made in roubles if the transfer is unsucc=
essful, adding that western sanctions freezing some of the Russian central =
bank=E2=80=99s assets were an attempt to force the country into an =E2=80=
=9Cartificial default=E2=80=9D on its $38.5bn of foreign currency bonds.</p=
></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If Russia can=E2=80=99t pay in d=
ollars and has to pay in rubles, does that count as a default? Yes, is the =
traditional answer; that is what <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusi=
ness.com/click/27048155.256604/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2Fy=
dGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMTUvcnVzc2lhLXRvLWRlZmF1bHQtaWYtZG9sbGFyLWNvdXBvbnMtcGF=
pZC1pbi1ydWJsZXMtZml0Y2gtc2F5cz9jbXBpZD1CQkQwMzE2MjJfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbW=
VkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMjAzMTYmdXRtX2Nhb=
XBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBdfa4e21a" itemprop=3D"Story=
Link" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; te=
xt-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-col=
or: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> Fitch Ra=
tings has said</a>, and presumably what other ratings agencies will say, an=
d also effectively what credit default swap contracts say.[2] I am not sure=
 how much this matters, insofar as, if Russia defaults, there is <a href=3D=
"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27048155.256604/aHR0cHM6Ly93=
d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMTUvYW1jLWJvdWdodC1=
hLWdvbGQtbWluZT9jbXBpZD1CQkQwMzE2MjJfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJn=
V0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMjAzMTYmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c=
3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBe42d1dc9" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> not that much its bondh=
olders can do about it</a>. The traditional remedies include (1) suing (whi=
ch will get you a judgment that you can=E2=80=99t enforce), (2) going to Mo=
scow to negotiate some sort of restructuring (which you can=E2=80=99t do, d=
ue to the sanctions) and (3) shouting =E2=80=9Cif you default you will be l=
ocked out of international capital markets!=E2=80=9D (which has already hap=
pened).=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Also to the extent Russia pay=
s its foreign bondholders in rubles =E2=80=A6=C2=A0can they =E2=80=A6=C2=A0=
get the rubles? In addition to the dollar bonds with payments due today, Ru=
ssia has ruble-denominated bonds held by foreign investors that had interes=
t payments due two weeks ago. Russia =E2=80=9Cpaid=E2=80=9D those coupons, =
in some abstract sense, but the payments <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloom=
bergbusiness.com/click/27048155.256604/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9u=
ZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMTUvcnVzc2lhLXRvLWRlZmF1bHQtaWYtZG9sbGFyLWNvdXB=
vbnMtcGFpZC1pbi1ydWJsZXMtZml0Y2gtc2F5cz9jbXBpZD1CQkQwMzE2MjJfTU9ORVlTVFVGRi=
Z1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMjAzMTYmd=
XRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cCdfa4e21a" itemprop=
=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
 did not get to investors</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=
Fitch=E2=80=99s latest release Tuesday also noted Russia=E2=80=99s =E2=80=
=9Cfailure to credit non-resident investors=E2=80=9D with the coupons for R=
ussian local-currency government bonds, known as OFZs, which were due on Ma=
rch 2.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CWe understand that Russia=
=E2=80=99s Ministry of Finance made these coupon payments on the 2024 OFZs =
to the National Settlement Depository, but they were not paid on to foreign=
 investors because of Central Bank of Russia restrictions,=E2=80=9D accordi=
ng to Fitch. =E2=80=9CThis will constitute a default if not cured within 30=
 days of the payments falling due.=E2=80=9D</p></blockquote><p style=3D"mar=
gin: 16px 0;">This seems to be mostly <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomber=
gbusiness.com/click/27048155.256604/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZnQuY29tL2NvbnRlbnQvMmM=
wZDdhOGItYTQ4Yi00Mjg3LWFjM2EtMzc2NjAzMzQ3YmEz/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cCd9ee=
f396" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" styl=
e=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width:=
 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-=
color: #ccc;">due to a Russian</a> =E2=80=9Ccentral bank ban on sending for=
eign currency abroad,=E2=80=9D though I suppose if you=E2=80=99re a U.S. in=
vestor or intermediary you might worry a little bit about=C2=A0<em>U.S.=C2=
=A0</em>sanctions restricting your ability to take or pass along=C2=A0ruble=
 payments from Russia. But broadly speaking the point seems to be:</p><ol s=
tyle=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=09<=
li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The U.S. might not allow Russia to pay its=
 bonds in dollars, and</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Russia migh=
t not=C2=A0allow its bondholders to get paid in rubles.</li></ol><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Seems like a default! In some sense the default is <a =
href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/27048155.256604/aHR0c=
HM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMTUvcnVzc2lhLXRv=
LWRlZmF1bHQtaWYtZG9sbGFyLWNvdXBvbnMtcGFpZC1pbi1ydWJsZXMtZml0Y2gtc2F5cz9jbXB=
pZD1CQkQwMzE2MjJfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2=
xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMjAzMTYmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8=
524a319a23cDdfa4e21a" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=
=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;"> imminent and historic</a>:</p><blockquote><p=
 style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The country would be in default if it hasn=E2=80=
=99t, within 30 days of a March 2 payment due date, =E2=80=9Ccured=E2=80=9D=
 its obligations with regard to ruble-denominated bond coupons that were du=
e earlier this month, Fitch said in a document Tuesday on how to understand=
 potential sovereign default events.=C2=A0That sets April 1 as a potential =
key date for the country, which hasn=E2=80=99t defaulted on local currency =
bonds since 1998 and last failed to meet foreign-debt obligations back in 1=
918, following the Bolshevik revolution.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin=
: 16px 0;">In another sense the default has already happened: Once sanction=
s made it impossible for many international investors to trade with Russia,=
 normal concepts like =E2=80=9Ca default will drive down the price of bonds=
=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Ca default will cut Russia off from international fina=
ncing=E2=80=9D=C2=A0<em>happened immediately</em>, so the eventual=C2=A0<em=
>actual=C2=A0</em>default has limited economic impact. And the usual=C2=A0<=
em>resolution=C2=A0</em>of a default =E2=80=94=C2=A0the defaulting country =
restructures its bonds, or disavows them, and enough time passes that it ev=
entually can come back to the market =E2=80=94=C2=A0seems impossible until=
=C2=A0Russia's invasion of=C2=A0Ukraine is resolved first. In a sense today=
 (or April 1) is an important day, but in another sense Russia defaulted on=
 its bonds the minute it invaded Ukraine and will be in default until the i=
nvasion ends.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Elsewhere Russia has a lot of=
 gold, but <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/2704815=
5.256604/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMT=
YvdGhlLTE0MC1iaWxsaW9uLXF1ZXN0aW9uLWNhbi1ydXNzaWEtc2VsbC1pdHMtaHVnZS1nb2xkL=
XBpbGU_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDMxNjIyX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291=
cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjIwMzE2JnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/60=
7f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBaad4a854" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemsc=
ope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impo=
rtant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-botto=
m-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> how useful is that</a>?</p><block=
quote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Bank of Russia expanded its gold reserve=
s almost sixfold since the mid-2000s, creating the world=E2=80=99s fifth-bi=
ggest stockpile that=E2=80=99s valued at about $140 billion. It=E2=80=99s t=
he type of asset it could sell to shore up the ruble, which has plunged as =
global economies isolate Russia following its invasion of Ukraine.</p><p st=
yle=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Doing so will be difficult. Sanctions forbid U.S., =
U.K. and European Union institutions from doing business with Russia=E2=80=
=99s central bank. Traders and banks are wary of buying the country=E2=80=
=99s bullion indirectly or using other currencies out of fear of reputation=
al damage or falling foul of penalties. And senators in Washington want sec=
ondary sanctions on anyone buying or selling Russian gold.</p><p style=3D"m=
argin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CThis is why they bought their gold, it was for a s=
ituation just like this,=E2=80=9D said Fergal O=E2=80=99Connor, a lecturer =
at Cork University Business School. =E2=80=9CBut if no one will trade it wi=
th you, it doesn=E2=80=99t matter.=E2=80=9D</p></blockquote><p style=3D"mar=
gin: 16px 0;">They could pay the coupons in gold? =E2=80=9CSend us a mailin=
g address and we=E2=80=99ll ship you the gold=E2=80=9D? I don=E2=80=99t kno=
w, probably not a great idea.</p><table class=3D"liveintent-flex-ad" border=

<tr>

DZkZWE4MmQ2YjE2NjE1Y2Y0NDIzM2JlNjIwNTI1JnA9MjczMDQ3NTkmc3RwZT1kZWZhdWx0Jg/6=
07f07ceef4b8524a319a23cHd9fccb7d" rel=3D"nofollow">
<img src=3D"https://sli.bloomberg.com/imp?s=3D825885&li=3D27048155&=

5px;">ESG</h2>
</td>

</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you are an investor interested in e=
nvironmental, social and governance issues, you have had to make some choic=
es about Russia in recent weeks. Maybe not that many choices. Various Russi=
an entities were subject to sanctions, many=C2=A0Russian securities were su=
spended in Europe and the U.S. and removed from indexes, the whole Moscow=
=C2=A0Exchange has been <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/=
click/27048155.256604/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzL=
zIwMjItMDMtMTIvcnVzc2lhbi1zdG9jay1tYXJrZXQtdHJhZGluZy1oYWx0LWV4dGVuZGVkLXRv=
LW1hcmNoLTE4P2NtcGlkPUJCRDAzMTYyMl9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXR=
tX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIyMDMxNiZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdH=
VmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB98dde4e9" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D=
"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: non=
e !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; borde=
r-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> shut down for weeks</a>; i=
t=E2=80=99s possible that for many of your Russian positions you had no cho=
ice at all beyond =E2=80=9Cwe have to sell this but also we can=E2=80=99t s=
ell it.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Still, in theory, as an ES=
G investor, you probably want to have a view on investing in (non-sanctione=
d, tradable) Russian assets. =E2=80=9CInvesting in Russia supports a war of=
 aggression against Ukraine so we won=E2=80=99t do it=E2=80=9D seems like t=
he leading ESG view right now, though I suppose others are also possible.</=
p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But how far do you take that view? If Russia=
n companies are off-limits for ESG investors, what about companies that inv=
est in Russia? Do business in Russia? Buy Russian oil? If you are an ESG in=
vestor that spends time (1) evaluating Western companies and investing only=
 in the ones that pollute the least, have the best social policies, etc. an=

ears ago, you could not go five minutes in financial services without heari=
ng the word =E2=80=9Cblockchain.=E2=80=9D Every financial product would one=
 day trade on =E2=80=9Cthe blockchain,=E2=80=9D which would revolutionize s=
ettlement and lead to huge efficiency gains. Everything would live on the b=
lockchain and be instantly legible to everyone at all times; every trade wo=
uld settle instantly; vast realms of back-office processes would disappear =
and be replaced by a single distributed ledger.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px=
 0;">The product that would most especially live on the blockchain=C2=A0was=
 leveraged loans. Leveraged loans are strange in that (1) they are a large,=
 important,=C2=A0frequently traded asset class, (2) they are kind of like h=
igh-yield bonds, which trade and settle in a pretty normal way, but (3) for=
 various historical and legal reasons they do=C2=A0<em>not=C2=A0</em>settle=
 like bonds; instead they can take two weeks to settle and sometimes you ha=
ve to use a fax machine. Putting stock trading on the blockchain might add =
some efficiency, but putting leveraged loans on the blockchain would add a=
=C2=A0<em>ton=C2=A0</em>of efficiency. Also millions of individuals trade s=
tocks, while a relatively small pool of banks and investors trade leveraged=
 loans, so it is just a bit easier to get everyone to agree to migrate them=
 to the blockchain. Leveraged loans were the lowest-hanging blockchain frui=
t, and people talked constantly about moving them to the blockchain.</p><p =
style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Now it is 2022 and here is Bloomberg=E2=80=99s Pa=

 #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> Old-School =

kquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The $1.38 trillion U.S. leveraged loan =
market is nearing an innovation that could finally shift back-office operat=
ions to a centralized system and away from investors having to manually tra=
ck their positions -- a process that can still include the occasional fax.<=
/p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Bank of America Corp., Citigroup Inc. and J=
PMorgan Chase & Co. on Wednesday unveiled the name of the new platform =
that will let lenders access data across their portfolios in one place and =
said that Credit Suisse Group AG, one of the top arrangers for leveraged lo=
ans, has joined the portal.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Called Versana,=
 the platform will formally launch around the middle of this year with term=
 loans, and then soon add revolving credit facilities and other services, s=
aid the company=E2=80=99s Chief Executive Officer Cynthia Sachs. =E2=80=A6<=
/p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Every loan has one bank that serves as the =
administrative agent and provides back-office record keeping. They notify l=
enders on a range of changes that can occur, such as an interest payment, a=
 company paying down debt early and amendments to the legal documentation.<=
/p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But those notifications are not standardize=
d, and investors often have to track a hodgepodge of messages from a number=
 of different banks. If there is a discrepancy, an investor has to call up,=
 email or instant message the bank to sort out why, for example, the cash f=
lows in their portfolio don=E2=80=99t match their expectations.=C2=A0</p></=
blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">No mention of blockchains! A centra=
lized system! To be fair this is for administrative notices, not settlement=
, but you get the idea. =E2=80=9CWe should have a nicer and more logical wa=

le have thought for a long time. In 2017 they expressed that sentiment by s=
aying =E2=80=9Ceverything will move to the blockchain.=E2=80=9D Now they ju=
st build a computer system.</p><table class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" borde=
